# RFC-0005: Evidence And Operator Journal Contract

> **Version:** 0.4.0 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** test

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0005:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0005c-summary"></a>

This RFC governs durable evidence: the file-first execution records that server, recipe, Eval, Bench, and image workflows write ([RFC-0003](../rfc/RFC-0003.md), [RFC-0007](../rfc/RFC-0007.md)), their unredacted-by-design posture, and the operator scratchpad journal that shares the record time axis. Workspace and source-identity terms are defined by [RFC-0002](../rfc/RFC-0002.md); measurement terms by [RFC-0004](../rfc/RFC-0004.md). [RFC-0001](../rfc/RFC-0001.md) holds the specification overview and the authority map.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0005:C-EVIDENCE] Records And Evidence (Normative) <a id="rfc-0005c-evidence"></a>

Inferlab MUST create a durable local execution record before the first external effect of a non-dry-run server, recipe, Eval, or Bench execution.

A long-running server record MUST preserve its lifecycle state until stop or recovery finalizes it.

A closed-loop recipe record MUST aggregate the selected cases and their server, measurement, failure, skip, and cleanup evidence.

Execution evidence MUST identify the producing Inferlab version, workspace revision, recipe identity, selected case identity and declaration order, resolved referenced definitions, effective settings, explicit overrides, framework integration identity, effective invocations, effective environment values, placement, endpoint selection, measurement results, and cleanup outcome when those facts apply.

Process commands and logs MUST be recorded when an external process starts.

A multi-role server record MUST additionally identify logical roles, declared and effective replica counts, replica identities, process-to-role and process-to-replica bindings, rank identities, local GPU groups, per-role settings and effective parallelism, per-process placement and launch evidence, each replica's serving entry point, directed role links, internal endpoints, routing or proxy evidence, the public workload endpoint, readiness, lifecycle handles, and cleanup outcomes.

A non-dry-run server record MUST identify, for each machine that hosts a serving process, the GPU hardware actually assigned on that machine — each assigned device's model name, total memory capacity, and device UUID — and that machine's driver version, captured from the executing machine at launch through the same launch path as the serving processes. A failed hardware probe MUST fail the launch before serving processes start, naming the machine and the probe error.

Profiling evidence MUST additionally identify capture targets, logical-role, replica, process, and rank bindings, profiler tool and control identities, storage, capture plans, semantic windows, range mappings, control actions, collection finalization, report references, verification outcomes, and failures when those facts apply.

Eval evidence MUST additionally identify the selected execution kind, effective request, result metrics, and raw artifacts, plus failure evidence when the Eval fails.

Built-in OpenAI smoke evidence MUST identify the built-in execution kind and producing Inferlab version.

Built-in OpenAI smoke evidence MUST NOT claim that an lm-eval or Bench runtime participated.

When an Eval uses the installed lm-eval runtime, its evidence MUST additionally identify the host platform, toolchain manifest and lock digests, Eval runtime and runner identities, process invocation, logs, and process outcome.

Bench evidence MUST additionally identify the host platform, toolchain manifest and lock digests, AIPerf and Bench runner identities, adaptive policy and normalization schema identities, exact executed case, resolved AIPerf configuration, native invocation, request counts, normalized metrics, raw artifacts, logs, process outcome, cleanup outcome, and failure evidence.

A manual Bench record MUST identify the target server record, its producing Inferlab version and serving snapshot, and the invoking Inferlab version and measurement workspace snapshot that supplied the Bench definition and overrides.

Dry-run output and execution records MUST preserve the actual resolved model locator, machine host, port, GPU assignment, command arguments, and environment values when those facts apply.

Inferlab MUST NOT perform value-level redaction or maintain a separate shareable record projection. Operators are responsible for access control, retention, and sharing of local records that may contain sensitive values.

A dirty-workspace execution record MUST preserve the baseline revision, dirty-state marker, effective source-content digest, and non-reproducible status.

A dirty-workspace execution record MUST NOT claim that checking out the baseline revision alone reproduces the execution.

A finalized record MUST remain inspectable without reloading the original workspace, contacting the original machine, or rerunning the operation.

Inferlab records MUST NOT require compatibility with record schemas or directory layouts that predate this contract.

**Rationale:** Records are the durable output used to reproduce, diagnose, and compare inference optimization work. Multi-role execution and profiling add behavior-affecting role, replica, GPU-group, process-rank, routing, and observation facts that cannot be reconstructed from a flat command list. External measurement-toolchain identity and process evidence belong only to measurements that use those boundaries; built-in measurement execution is identified by the producing Inferlab version and selected execution kind. A manual attachment can use a measurement definition newer than the running server snapshot, so both producer identities and snapshots remain visible. Preserving exact effective inputs is simpler and more useful than maintaining a lossy redacted projection. GPU hardware identity is an execution-time fact: drivers upgrade and devices get replaced, so an inspection-time snapshot cannot describe this run; probing through the launch path keeps one authority, answers which hardware served a finalized record without contacting the machine, and makes a bindings-versus-reality mismatch loud at first use instead of surfacing later as a CUDA error.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0005:C-SCRATCHPAD-JOURNAL] Operator Scratchpad Journal (Normative) <a id="rfc-0005c-scratchpad-journal"></a>

Inferlab MUST provide an operator scratchpad journal: one workspace-local append-only stream of entries per workspace, stored as machine state under the workspace state directory.

A journal entry MUST identify its creation timestamp, its author, its free text, an optional topic label, and zero or more referenced execution records. Entries MUST be appended in creation order, and the scratchpad surface MUST NOT edit or delete an appended entry.

Entry timestamps MUST use the same UTC format as execution record identifiers so entries and records order on one time axis.

Appending MUST require no prior setup: the journal and any topic exist by first use, a topic carries no lifecycle state, and no workspace-level mode routes entries — an entry's topic is stated on the entry itself or absent. Entries without a topic form the common stream.

Appending an entry MUST validate that each referenced execution record exists and MUST NOT mutate a referenced record.

The journal rendering MUST present entries chronologically with a one-line summary for each referenced record and MUST support restricting the view to one topic. A default view MAY limit itself to recent entries, but it MUST state how many earlier entries are omitted, and a complete view MUST be available.

Scratchpad state MUST be excluded from workspace source identity and MUST NOT become an input to resolution, execution, or record content.

Scratchpad state MUST remain inspectable as plain files without the Inferlab binary.

**Rationale:** Journal writes compete with the experiment itself, so the write path carries no ceremony and no mode. A sticky active-thread designation is only convenient when narratives do not interleave — exactly when routing is unneeded — and silently misroutes append-only entries when several efforts (for example, multiple models validated side by side in one workspace) do interleave; an explicit per-entry topic is the only routing that cannot land an entry in the wrong narrative, and an absent topic costs nothing. Reading is rare but resume-oriented, so the default view leads with the recent tail. Entries share the record-identifier time axis so records and narrative interleave into one history without a second authority: records own execution facts, the journal owns the narrative, and neither feeds resolution. Scratchpad state stays out of source identity for the same reason records do, and one line per entry under a short exclusive file lock — the timestamp taken inside the lock — keeps concurrent entries whole and in time-axis order on any filesystem, including network mounts where bare O_APPEND guarantees neither; readers take the shared lock, so a partially written line is never observed.

*Since: v0.1.0*
